primary_drivers: Preview article only — no new policy information
No surprise data or guidance shift contained in headline
headlines: Here's everything to expect when the Fed issues its latest interest rate decision Wednesday

macro_events.event_summary: Pentagon AI chief publicly confirms DOD is expanding use of Google's AI models, framing multi-vendor sourcing as policy and signaling reduced reliance on any single AI provider (reportedly after an Anthropic blacklist).
primary_drivers: DOD AI procurement diversification away from single-vendor reliance
Google Cloud/Gemini gaining federal defense workload share
Anthropic losing a marquee federal customer relationship

beneficiaries.rationale: Direct named beneficiary — Pentagon AI chief explicitly confirmed expanded DOD use of Google models. Adds to Google Public Sector / GCP defense revenue and validates Gemini for classified/government workloads, a credential historically dominated by MSFT and AWS.

Palantir's Maven, TITAN and AIP deployments are the integration layer that operationalizes third-party LLMs (including Google's) inside DOD. More approved models flowing into DOD = more Palantir seat/usage expansion, and Palantir already has a Google Cloud partnership.

Booz Allen is the largest AI services integrator inside DOD; multi-vendor model strategy increases consulting/integration scope as the agency must stand up parallel pipelines for Google, OpenAI/Microsoft and others.
Leidos holds large DOD digital modernization contracts and benefits from any expansion of approved-model catalog requiring integration, security accreditation, and deployment work.

losers.rationale: Microsoft (via Azure OpenAI and its IL5/IL6 accreditations) has been the default DOD frontier-model provider. Pentagon AI chief explicitly stating reliance on one model is 'never a good thing' is a direct policy signal that Azure/OpenAI's incumbent share will be diluted by Google.

headlines: Pentagon AI chief confirms DOD's expanded use of Google, says reliance on one model 'never a good thing'

macro_events.event_summary: A MarketWatch opinion/analysis piece speculates which two countries are most likely to next exit OPEC's orbit, suggesting further fragmentation of the cartel.
primary_drivers: Speculative commentary, not a confirmed policy event
Potential erosion of OPEC supply discipline → bearish long-term oil price implication
No immediate change to production quotas or fundamentals
headlines: These two countries are the most likely to leave OPEC’s orbit next
